retrieved_docs.title: Hot Flashes -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The exact pathogenesis of hot flashes is unknown, but studies indicate that VMS (vasomotor symptoms) results from a central thermoregulatory function defect. [1] The physiological changes accompanying VMS are clear and well documented, yet several hypotheses about the mechanism of action exist. Various hormones and neurotransmitters modulate vasomotor symptoms, most importantly, estrogen. The thinking is that ovarian estrogen withdrawal is the initial mechanism leading to hot flashes. Norepinephrine and serotonin also have a role in the defect in thermoregulation with VMS, which is why SSRIs and SNRIs have been used to treat VMS. [1] Research documents that plasma levels of norepinephrine metabolites rise before and during a hot flash. [1] Peripheral vasodilation occurs, increasing to 10 to 15 degrees C in the fingers and toes. [3] A rise in systolic blood pressure and heart rate also occurs. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Temperature Regulation -- Clinical Significance -- Hot Flashes
retrieved_docs.content: As a woman ages, they will undergo menopause and typically experience hot flashes caused by estrogen withdrawal. Of note, no consistent correlation has been found between plasma estrogen levels and the severity of a woman's symptoms. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Hot Flashes -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hot flashes are sudden-onset, spontaneous, and episodic sensations of warmth usually felt on the chest, neck, and face immediately followed by sweating. They are the most common reason women seek medical care during the perimenopausal period, especially if the symptoms impair their quality of life. The onset of hot flashes can be associated with perspiration, heart palpitations, headache, weakness, fatigue, faintness, and anxiety, and warm environments, hot drinks, or emotional stress can trigger them. This activity reviews the presentation, evaluation, and management of hot flashes and stresses the role of an interprofessional team approach to the care of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Temperature Regulation -- Clinical Significance -- Hot Flashes
retrieved_docs.content: Robert Freedman, a professor of psychiatry and obstetrics and gynecology at Wayne State University, has found that women who experience hot flashes tend to demonstrate a more narrow hypothalamic temperature zone between the upper and lower threshold temperatures involved in heat loss and heat production, respectively, which leads to an increased likelihood of crossing these thresholds and developing sweats and chills associated with hot flashes. A pre-menopausal woman has an interthreshold zone of about 0.4°C, in which temperature fluctuations between 0 and 0.4°C will not trigger compensatory chills, flushing, or sweating. However, in a menopausal woman, this threshold zone is significantly reduced, leading to even minute fluctuations in body temperature initiating thermoregulatory responses. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Hot Flashes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hot flashes are sudden-onset, spontaneous, and episodic sensations of warmth usually felt on the chest, neck, and face immediately followed by sweating. They are the most common reason women seek medical care during the perimenopausal period, especially if the symptoms impair quality of life. [1] The onset of hot flashes can be associated with perspiration, heart palpitations, headache, weakness, fatigue, faintness, and anxiety, and warm environments, hot drinks, or emotional stress can trigger them. Hot flashes are variable in terms of duration, severity, and frequency. Frequency and severity can increase during the transition to menopause and peak approximately 1 year after the final menstrual period. [2] They can persist for 6 months to several years, usually decreasing in frequency and intensity over time after the final menstrual period. [2] On average, they last less than 5 minutes. The average frequency varies from 10 times daily to several times weekly. [2] The mean duration is 1.2 years. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Irregular menstrual cycles, hot flashes, and night sweats are predominant during the perimenopausal state. After menopause, genitourinary symptoms like vulvovaginal atrophy and dryness predominate. Most of the symptoms have been difficult to establish a true relationship with menopause and response to treatment with estrogen, but vasomotor and genitourinary symptoms show a good response to hormone replacement therapy. Postmenopausal women also have symptoms of sexual dysfunction, including decreased libido, reduced frequency of sexual thoughts, and fantasies.

retrieved_docs.title: Menopause (Nursing) -- Nursing Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Altered sleep Irritable Depressed mood Thinning bones Low libido Low self-esteem Muscle and joint pain Excess sweating Skin dryness Painful sexual intercourse Anxiety Hot flashes Mood swings

retrieved_docs.title: Menopause -- History and Physical -- Psychogenic Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Up to 70% of women experience psychogenic symptoms associated with perimenopause and menopause. [14] These symptoms may include anger/irritability, anxiety/tension, depression, loss of concentration, and loss of self-esteem/confidence. Sleep apnea, insomnia, and restless leg syndrome may cause further sleep disturbances that are not explained purely by night sweats. Because there are estrogen receptors in a variety of regions of the brain in areas that regulate mood and cognition, it is not surprising that a decrease in levels of estrogen affects mood. In addition, estrogen has mediating effects on serotonin and noradrenaline transmission, both of which may benefit mood. [14] Alternatively, some mood changes may be attributed to changes in personal life and social circumstances rather than hormonal changes. [5] The risk of depressive symptoms and a higher level of depressive symptom severity are noted in perimenopause as compared to premenopausal women. Vasomotor symptoms and a variety of other potential reasons that have not yet been well-delineated may be associated with this increase in depression seen especially in perimenopausal but also in menopausal women. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- History and Physical -- Clinical Vignette 1
retrieved_docs.content: Sheila is recently postmenopausal and has a history of hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. She reports that she had worsening hot flashes that interfere with her sleep. Six weeks ago, her gynecologist tapered her escitalopram from 20 mg daily to 10 mg daily and started venlafaxine extended release at 37.5 mg daily. Two weeks ago, escitalopram was discontinued, and venlafaxine was increased to 75 mg daily. She reports taking this medication as prescribed. Despite a marked improvement in hot flashes, she continued to have difficulty sleeping. Other symptoms include heightened irritability, anhedonia, increased appetite, poor concentration, and decreased energy. She finds meaning in her work and family, though she notes “fleeting” thoughts of “what’s the point of going on?” Sheila denied current thoughts of suicide, any recent or present suicide plan or intention, or any suicide preparations. She feels guilty about feeling sad when her family and colleagues are so supportive of her. She reports taking a few sick days from work due to fatigue, but has not had any significant problems at work. Her affect is constricted. There is no psychomotor retardation. Sheila denies auditory or visual hallucinations and does not appear paranoid or delusional.

retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The significant symptoms of postmenopausal syndrome are vasomotor (hot flashes), vaginal and urinary (termed as GSM-genitourinary syndrome of menopause), insomnia, and mood. Hot flashes are a sudden sensation of heat centered on the upper chest and face that rapidly becomes generalized and lasts two to four minutes with associated perspiration. Palpitations, chills, shivering, and a feeling of anxiety may be present. This frequency of symptoms ranges from one to several times per day. It is also reported most commonly at night. The average duration of hot flashes is about 5.2 years but can also persist as long as 20 years. [2] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Autoimmune Disease and Gonadal Failure -- History and Physical -- Key Symptoms and Physical Signs of Gonadal Failure in Females
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms Irregular menstrual periods (oligomenorrhea) or absence of periods (amenorrhea) Abdominal cramping, bloating, nausea, and vomiting Vaginal dryness and decreased libido Mood changes, such as depression and anxiety, and sleep disturbances Vasomotor instability, such as hot flashes and night sweats Altered urinary frequency Dyspareunia [1] Physical examination findings Breast atrophy Loss of pubic and axillary hair Thinning of the vaginal epithelium and decreased vaginal lubrication Dry skin [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Hot Flashes -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of hot flashes. Determine the history and physical exam findings typically seen in patients with hot flashes. Identify the management options for hot flashes. Communicate the modalities to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members in order to improve outcomes for patients affected by hot flashes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Temperature Regulation -- Clinical Significance -- Hot Flashes
retrieved_docs.content: Careful and accurate monitoring of core body temperature is of great importance in patients with thermoregulatory dysfunction to provide appropriate treatment, improve their quality of life, and prevent serious complications from arising.

retrieved_docs.title: Claustrophobia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Sweating and chills Increased heart rate and high blood pressure Dizziness and lightheadedness Dry mouth Hyperventilation Hot flashes Nausea Headache Numbness A choking sensation Chest tightness/pain and difficulty breathing An urge to urinate Confusion Not all people with claustrophobia will present with all these features, as the presentation can be variable.

retrieved_docs.title: Heat Illness -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: This process is monitored and affected through sensory receptors. Thermoreceptors found in the skin, organs, and spinal cord relay information via afferent nerve fibers to the preoptic area of the hypothalamus. [4] This response causes autonomic and behavioral changes that help correct body temperature back to baseline. Physiologic changes include vasodilation of blood vessels, with the majority of flow occurring primarily in the peripheral arteriovenous anastomoses (AVA). The highest concentration of AVAs are present in the glabrous tissues, including the hands, feet, and face, and, as such, are important areas to address during treatment with rapid cooling. [5] Other physiologic changes include an increased rate of sweat production via sympathetic cholinergic receptors and decreased metabolism leading to decreased internal heat production. [6] It also elicits behavioral modifications, but these are under voluntary control. Examples of these include the desire to seek shade or shelter, use air-conditioning or forced convection with fans, and loosen clothing or shed insulating layers. As these are under voluntary control, individuals are at an increased risk for heat illness when they ignore these impulses either consciously or subconsciously. An example of this is athletes who push themselves to train in hot conditions or in elderly residents who may not possess access to air-conditioning units during heat waves or the means to evacuate from an unsafe environment. [7] Comorbidities, mental status changes, and medications may also play a role in limiting a person's response to exposure to a hot environment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2693
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymology is recognized as an allowed qualifier for both anxiety (القلق) and nonspecific chest pain, indicating that enzymatic assessments or markers may be relevant when evaluating these conditions. Additionally, nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with anxiety, suggesting that patients experiencing anxiety may present with or be linked to chest pain that lacks a specific diagnostic cause. This interconnected knowledge underscores the importance of considering enzymatic factors in the clinical assessment of anxiety‑related chest discomfort and highlights the relationship between psychological stress and ambiguous thoracic symptoms in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2835
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with القلق (anxiety), indicating that anxiety often underlies or contributes to this vague thoracic discomfort. In addition, nonspecific chest pain shares symptom similarity with ألم بطني (abdominal pain), suggesting that patients may experience overlapping or confusing pain sensations between the chest and the abdomen. Recognizing both the psychological link to anxiety and the potential overlap with abdominal pain is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) and nonspecific chest pain are clinically associated, indicating that patients experiencing anxiety may also present with nonspecific chest pain and vice versa. Pharmacotherapy is recognized as an appropriate and permitted treatment option for both anxiety and nonspecific chest pain, highlighting the relevance of medication management in addressing these interrelated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2681
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension is clinically associated with a range of symptoms and conditions. Patients with hypertension often experience nausea and vomiting, indicating that these gastrointestinal symptoms can be a manifestation of elevated blood pressure. Surgical intervention such as partial thyroidectomy is also linked to hypertension, and this procedure naturally co‑occurs with the thyroid gland itself, reflecting the close anatomical and functional relationship between thyroid disease and blood‑pressure regulation. Disorders of the autonomic nervous system have a broader relationship with hypertension, underscoring the role of autonomic dysregulation in the development and maintenance of high blood pressure. Diffuse scleroderma, a systemic connective‑tissue disease, is related to hypertension, highlighting the cardiovascular impact of widespread fibrosis and vascular involvement. Chronic atrial fibrillation is another condition clinically associated with hypertension, emphasizing the interplay between sustained high blood pressure and cardiac rhythm disturbances. Finally, hypertension is frequently associated with a carotid bruit, a vascular sound indicating turbulent flow in the carotid artery, which serves as a useful clinical sign of underlying arterial disease. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, early detection of comorbidities, and integrated management of patients with hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 603
retrieved_docs.content: Syncope, which is classified as a type of loss of consciousness (فقدان الوعي), is clinically associated with chest constriction (خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ). Chest constriction is also associated with spinal rigidity (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ). These interconnections indicate that patients experiencing syncope may report a sensation of tightness in the chest, and that spinal rigidity may be linked to the same chest symptom, underscoring the importance of evaluating both thoracic and spinal findings when assessing episodes of loss of consciousness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 482
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق), fatigue (التَّعَب) and nonspecific chest pain are closely interrelated clinical entities. Fatigue is described as a defining characteristic of anxiety, indicating that patients who experience anxiety often report persistent tiredness. Both anxiety and fatigue are clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain, suggesting that these symptoms frequently co‑occur in the same patient presentations. In addition, nonspecific chest pain is clinically linked to aortic stenosis (تضيق الأبهر) and to oral thrush (داء المبيضات الفموي), and fatigue shares the same clinical associations with aortic stenosis and oral thrush. Enzymology is recognized as an allowed qualifier for anxiety, nonspecific chest pain, and fatigue, meaning that enzymatic studies or descriptors may be appropriately applied when evaluating or documenting these conditions. Understanding these connections aids clinicians in recognizing overlapping symptom patterns and in considering underlying cardiac or infectious etiologies during assessment and management.

Clinically, both nonspecific chest pain and fatigue are associated with oral thrush داء المبيضات الفموي, suggesting an infectious contribution to these symptoms. Female infertility is linked to endometrial implantation pathology. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 978
retrieved_docs.content: Increased physiological findings are interpreted as indicative of essential hypertension and may also be understood as a sign of tachycardia (تسرع القلب). Essential hypertension itself is interpreted through measurements of blood pressure (ضغط الدم). Comprehensive evaluation of cardiovascular function—such as tilt‑table testing combined with continuous ECG monitoring and intermittent blood pressure monitoring, with or without pharmacological intervention—includes blood pressure as a central component of the assessment. Blood pressure (ضغط الدم) is recognized to have a relationship with low‑blood‑pressure states (ضغط الدم المنخفض), highlighting the importance of distinguishing between elevated and reduced arterial pressures in clinical evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق), fatigue (التَّعَب), nonspecific chest pain, and oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) are interrelated clinical concepts. Enzymology is recognized as an allowed qualifier for anxiety, nonspecific chest pain, and fatigue, indicating that enzymatic assessments or interpretations may be relevant when evaluating these conditions. Fatigue is described as having anxiety as a defining characteristic, suggesting that feelings of anxiety are integral to the experience of fatigue. Clinically, nonspecific chest pain is associated both with anxiety and with oral candidiasis, while fatigue is also associated with oral candidiasis. These relationships highlight overlapping symptom patterns and potential shared pathophysiological mechanisms, underscoring the importance of a comprehensive evaluation that considers enzymological factors, anxiety, fatigue, chest discomfort, and possible oral fungal infection in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.
